faqs.q: Can I get a home loan on ₹25,000 salary?
faqs.a: Yes, several banks including SBI, Bank of Baroda, and PNB approve home loans for applicants earning ₹25,000 per month. At this salary, you can expect eligibility of ₹12–18 Lakh depending on the bank's FOIR and interest rate.

faqs.q: Which bank gives home loan on lowest salary?
faqs.a: SBI and Bank of Baroda have some of the lowest minimum income requirements for home loans, starting from ₹15,000–₹20,000 per month. Actual eligibility depends on your credit score and existing EMIs.

faqs.q: How much home loan can I get on ₹20,000 salary?
faqs.a: On a ₹20,000 monthly salary with no existing EMIs, you can typically get a home loan of ₹8–12 Lakh at current interest rates with a 20-year tenure. Adding a co-applicant can increase this amount significantly.

faqs.q: How to increase home loan eligibility with low salary?
faqs.a: You can increase eligibility by: adding a working spouse or family member as co-applicant, clearing all existing loans and credit card dues, choosing a longer tenure (up to 30 years), and maintaining a CIBIL score above 750.

faqs.q: Is PMAY available for low salary home loan applicants?
faqs.a: Yes, Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) offers interest subsidy of ₹2.35–₹2.67 Lakh for EWS (income up to ₹3 Lakh/year) and LIG (income up to ₹6 Lakh/year) categories. Most banks process PMAY-linked home loans.

faqs.q: Can I get a personal loan with 600 CIBIL score?
faqs.a: Yes, some NBFCs like Bajaj Finserv and Tata Capital may approve personal loans with a CIBIL score of 600–650. However, the interest rate will be higher (16–24% p.a.) compared to applicants with 750+ scores.

faqs.q: Which bank gives personal loan on lowest CIBIL score?
faqs.a: NBFCs are generally more flexible than banks. Bajaj Finserv, Tata Capital, and L&T Finance consider applicants with lower credit scores. Among banks, IDFC First Bank and IndusInd Bank may approve with scores around 650.

faqs.q: How to get a personal loan with bad credit history?
faqs.a: Options include: applying with NBFCs that have flexible criteria, taking a secured personal loan against FD or gold, applying with a co-applicant who has good credit, or getting a small-ticket loan to rebuild your score first.

faqs.q: What interest rate will I get with low CIBIL score?
faqs.a: With a CIBIL score of 600–650, expect personal loan interest rates of 16–24% p.a. This is significantly higher than the 10–12% offered to applicants with 750+ scores. Improving your score before applying can save lakhs in interest.

faqs.q: How long does it take to improve CIBIL score for a personal loan?
faqs.a: It typically takes 3–6 months of consistent on-time payments, low credit utilization (below 30%), and no new credit inquiries to see a meaningful improvement. Clearing overdue accounts can boost your score by 50–100 points.

faqs.q: What is the maximum LTV ratio for loan against property?

faqs.q: Is loan against property better than a personal loan?

faqs.q: What documents are needed for loan against property?

faqs.q: How long does LAP approval take?

faqs.q: How to check CIBIL score for free?
faqs.a: You can check your CIBIL score for free through: (1) CIBIL's official website (1 free report per year), (2) Your bank's net banking or mobile app (SBI YONO, HDFC, ICICI), (3) Third-party apps like Paytm, PhonePe, or BankBazaar.

faqs.q: What CIBIL score is needed for a loan?
faqs.a: Most banks require a minimum CIBIL score of 700 for personal and home loans. A score of 750+ gets you the best interest rates. For gold loans, the requirement may be as low as 575. NBFCs may approve loans with scores as low as 600–650.

faqs.q: Does checking CIBIL score reduce it?
faqs.a: No, checking your own CIBIL score (soft inquiry) does not reduce it. Only hard inquiries by banks when you apply for a loan or credit card can slightly reduce your score. Multiple hard inquiries in a short period can lower your score by 5–10 points.

faqs.q: How to improve CIBIL score quickly?
faqs.a: Pay all EMIs and credit card bills on time, keep credit utilization below 30%, avoid multiple loan applications in a short period, maintain a healthy mix of secured and unsecured loans, and dispute any errors in your CIBIL report.

faqs.q: How often is CIBIL score updated?
faqs.a: Banks report your credit data to CIBIL every 30–45 days. Your CIBIL score typically updates within 30–45 days of any change in your credit behavior like paying off a loan or missing an EMI.

faqs.q: What documents are required for a home loan?
faqs.a: Basic documents include: PAN card, Aadhaar card, salary slips (3 months), bank statements (6 months), Form 16/ITR (2 years), property documents (sale agreement, title deed), approved building plan, and passport-size photographs.

faqs.q: What additional documents do self-employed applicants need?
faqs.a: Self-employed applicants need: ITR for last 3 years, business registration/GST certificate, audited financial statements, profit & loss statement, balance sheet, and business bank statements for last 12 months.

faqs.q: What property documents are needed for a home loan?
faqs.a: Property documents include: sale agreement or allotment letter, title deed or conveyance deed, approved building plan, occupancy certificate (for ready property), land revenue receipts, encumbrance certificate, and NOC from housing society.

faqs.q: Can I apply for a home loan without ITR?
faqs.a: Most banks require ITR for home loan approval. However, some banks accept salary certificates, Form 16, or bank statements as alternative income proof for salaried employees. Self-employed applicants generally need ITR.

faqs.q: How long are home loan documents valid?
faqs.a: Salary slips and bank statements should be from the last 3–6 months. ITR copies should be from the last 2–3 financial years. Property documents should be current and clear of any legal disputes. Some banks may ask for updated documents if processing takes longer.

pages.title: How to Improve CIBIL Score — 10 Proven Tips (2026)
pages.description: Learn how to improve your CIBIL score fast with 10 actionable tips. Understand score ranges (300-900), common myths, and how long each improvement takes in 2026.
pages.keywords: how to improve cibil score, improve credit score india, increase cibil score, cibil score improvement tips 2026, boost cibil score fast
pages.heroSub: 10 proven, actionable tips to boost your CIBIL score and unlock better loan rates. Updated for 2026.

faqs.q: How long does it take to improve CIBIL score from 600 to 750?
faqs.a: Improving your CIBIL score from 600 to 750 typically takes 6–12 months of disciplined credit behaviour. Paying all EMIs and credit card bills on time, reducing credit utilization below 30%, and avoiding new hard inquiries are the fastest levers. Clearing any overdue or settled accounts can accelerate the process.

faqs.q: Does closing a credit card improve CIBIL score?
faqs.a: No, closing a credit card usually hurts your score. It reduces your total available credit limit, which increases your credit utilization ratio. It also shortens your credit history length. Instead of closing old cards, keep them active with small purchases and timely payments.

faqs.q: Can I improve my CIBIL score without a credit card?
faqs.a: Yes. You can build or improve your CIBIL score by taking a small secured loan (like a gold loan or loan against FD), paying all existing EMIs on time, and ensuring utility bill payments are reported to credit bureaus. A credit-builder loan from select NBFCs is another option.

faqs.q: How many points can CIBIL score increase in one month?
faqs.a: In a single month, your CIBIL score can increase by 10–30 points if you clear overdue payments, reduce credit card balances significantly, or get errors removed from your report. Large jumps of 50–100 points typically require 3–6 months of consistent effort.

faqs.q: Does checking my own CIBIL score lower it?
faqs.a: No. Checking your own CIBIL score is a soft inquiry and does not affect your score. Only hard inquiries made by banks or NBFCs when you apply for a loan or credit card can reduce your score by 5–10 points per inquiry.

guideContent.text: What Is a CIBIL Score?

A CIBIL score is a three-digit number between 300 and 900 assigned by TransUnion CIBIL, India's oldest credit bureau. Banks and NBFCs use this score to evaluate your creditworthiness before approving any loan or credit card application. A higher score signals lower risk to lenders, which translates into faster approvals, higher loan amounts, and lower interest rates.
CIBIL Score Ranges and What They Mean

Most banks consider 750 and above as the benchmark for premium loan offers. If your score is below 700, you may still get a loan from NBFCs, but at significantly higher interest rates. Anything below 600 makes it very difficult to secure unsecured credit.
10 Proven Tips to Improve Your CIBIL Score
1. Pay All EMIs and Credit Card Bills on Time

Payment history is the single most important factor, accounting for roughly 35% of your CIBIL score. Even one missed or late payment can drop your score by 50–100 points. Set up auto-debit or payment reminders to ensure you never miss a due date. Timeline: immediate impact within 30–45 days of consistent on-time payments.
2. Keep Credit Utilization Below 30%

Credit utilization ratio is the percentage of your total credit limit that you are using. If your credit card limit is ₹1 Lakh, try to keep outstanding balances below ₹30,000 at any point. High utilization signals credit hunger to bureaus. Timeline: 1–2 billing cycles (30–60 days).
3. Avoid Multiple Loan Applications in a Short Period

Every time you apply for a loan or credit card, the lender makes a hard inquiry on your CIBIL report. Multiple hard inquiries within a short span suggest financial distress and can reduce your score by 5–10 points each. Space out applications by at least 3–6 months. Timeline: hard inquiries stay on your report for 2 years, but their impact fades after 6–12 months.
4. Maintain a Healthy Credit Mix

CIBIL rewards borrowers who demonstrate the ability to handle different types of credit. A mix of secured loans (home loan, car loan) and unsecured credit (personal loan, credit card) shows responsible borrowing. Avoid having only unsecured credit in your profile. Timeline: 3–6 months to reflect positively.
5. Do Not Close Old Credit Cards

The length of your credit history matters. Older credit accounts contribute positively to your score. Closing an old credit card reduces your average credit age and also lowers your total available credit limit, increasing utilization. Keep old cards active with small periodic transactions. Timeline: impact is immediate upon closure.
6. Clear Overdue and Settled Accounts

If you have any accounts marked as overdue, settled, or written off, these are severe negative marks. Contact the lender and pay the full outstanding amount to get the status changed to closed. A settled account (where you paid less than the full amount) is almost as damaging as a default. Timeline: 30–60 days after clearance for the update to reflect.
7. Review Your CIBIL Report for Errors

Errors in your credit report are more common than you think. Wrong account details, duplicate entries, or incorrectly reported late payments can drag your score down unfairly. Download your free annual CIBIL report from myscore.cibil.com and dispute any inaccuracies. Timeline: CIBIL resolves disputes within 30 days.
8. Become an Authorized User on a Good Account

If a family member has an excellent credit history, ask them to add you as an authorized user on their credit card. Their positive payment history and low utilization will reflect on your report too, giving your score a boost. Timeline: 1–2 billing cycles (30–60 days).
9. Opt for a Secured Credit Card or Credit-Builder Loan

If you have no credit history or a very poor score, start with a secured credit card (backed by a fixed deposit) or a credit-builder loan. These products are designed to help you build a positive credit trail with minimal risk. Several banks and NBFCs offer secured cards with limits starting at ₹10,000–₹25,000. Timeline: 3–6 months of usage to see meaningful score improvement.
10. Limit Total Debt Relative to Income

Keep your total EMI obligations (including the new loan you are planning) below 40–50% of your monthly income. This is known as the Fixed Obligation to Income Ratio (FOIR). A lower FOIR signals to both lenders and credit bureaus that you are not over-leveraged. Timeline: ongoing discipline; impact builds over 3–6 months.
Common CIBIL Score Myths

items: Myth: Checking your own CIBIL score reduces it. Fact: Self-checks are soft inquiries and have zero impact.
Myth: Paying the minimum due on credit cards is enough. Fact: While it avoids a late-payment mark, carrying forward balances increases utilization and costs you heavy interest.
Myth: Higher income means higher CIBIL score. Fact: CIBIL does not consider your income. It only tracks your borrowing and repayment behaviour.

Myth: Closing a loan early always helps your score. Fact: Prepaying a loan is financially smart, but closing your only active loan removes your active credit line, which can temporarily dip your score.
Myth: CIBIL is the only credit bureau in India. Fact: There are four RBI-licensed bureaus — CIBIL (TransUnion), Experian, Equifax, and CRIF High Mark. Most banks primarily use CIBIL.

guideContent.text: How Long Does Each Improvement Take?

pages.title: Loan Against Property (LAP) — Complete Guide 2026
pages.description: Complete guide to Loan Against Property in India 2026. Learn about LAP eligibility, documents, interest rates, LTV ratio, and how LAP compares to personal loans.
pages.keywords: loan against property guide, LAP loan guide, loan against property eligibility, LAP vs personal loan, loan against property documents 2026
pages.heroSub: Everything you need to know about LAP: eligibility, documents, interest rates, LTV ratio, and when to choose it over a personal loan.

faqs.q: What is the maximum loan amount I can get against my property?
faqs.a: The maximum loan amount depends on your property's market value and the bank's Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio. Most banks offer 50–65% of the property's market value for residential properties and 40–55% for commercial properties. For example, if your property is valued at ₹1 Crore, you can typically get ₹50–65 Lakh as LAP.

faqs.q: Can I get a loan against property if the property is jointly owned?
faqs.a: Yes, but all co-owners of the property must be co-applicants or provide their consent and sign the loan agreement. The bank will require identity and address proof from all property owners, along with a joint declaration authorizing the mortgage.

faqs.q: What happens if I default on a loan against property?
faqs.a: If you default on LAP repayments, the bank will first send notices and try to restructure the loan. If you continue to default, the bank can initiate legal proceedings under the SARFAESI Act to seize and auction your property to recover the outstanding amount. This process typically takes 3–6 months after formal notice.

faqs.q: Can I take a loan against property that already has an existing home loan?
faqs.a: Yes, this is possible through a top-up loan or a second charge loan, but the combined LTV across both loans must remain within the bank's limits. Some banks allow a second mortgage if there is sufficient equity in the property after accounting for the outstanding home loan balance.

faqs.q: Is the interest paid on loan against property tax-deductible?
faqs.a: Tax benefits depend on the end-use of the loan. If the LAP funds are used for business purposes, the interest paid is deductible as a business expense. If used for home purchase or renovation, you can claim deduction under Section 24(b) up to ₹2 Lakh per year. No tax benefit is available if the funds are used for personal expenses.

guideContent.text: What Is a Loan Against Property (LAP)?

A Loan Against Property (LAP), also known as a mortgage loan, is a secured loan where you pledge your residential, commercial, or industrial property as collateral to borrow funds. Since the bank holds your property as security, LAP interest rates are significantly lower than unsecured loans like personal loans. You retain ownership and can continue to use the property while repaying the loan.

LAP is popular for funding large expenses such as business expansion, higher education, medical emergencies, debt consolidation, or even purchasing another property. Loan amounts typically range from ₹5 Lakh to ₹10 Crore, with tenures extending up to 15–20 years.
LAP Eligibility Criteria
Banks evaluate several factors when processing a LAP application. Meeting these criteria improves your chances of approval and may help you negotiate better terms.

items: Age: 25–65 years (at loan maturity). Some banks accept applicants from 21 years.
Income: Minimum ₹25,000–₹40,000 per month for salaried applicants. Self-employed applicants need minimum annual turnover of ₹10–15 Lakh.
CIBIL Score: 650 and above. A score of 750+ qualifies you for the best rates.
Property Ownership: The property must be in the applicant's name or jointly held (all owners must be co-applicants).
Property Age: The property should not be older than 30–40 years (varies by bank). Under-construction properties are generally not accepted.
Employment Stability: At least 2–3 years of total work experience for salaried applicants, or 3+ years of business existence for self-employed applicants.
Existing Obligations: Your total EMIs (including the proposed LAP) should not exceed 50–60% of your monthly income (FOIR limit).

guideContent.text: Documents Required for LAP
Identity and Address Proof

items: PAN Card (mandatory)
Aadhaar Card
Passport / Voter ID / Driving License
Passport-size photographs (2–4 copies)

guideContent.text: Income Documents (Salaried)

items: Salary slips (last 3 months)
Bank statements (last 6 months showing salary credits)
Form 16 or ITR (last 2 years)
Employment proof or appointment letter

guideContent.text: Income Documents (Self-Employed)

items: ITR with computation of income (last 3 years)
Audited balance sheet and P&L statement (last 3 years)
Business registration or GST certificate
Bank statements (last 12 months)

guideContent.text: Property Documents

items: Original title deed or sale deed
Encumbrance certificate (last 13–30 years)
Property tax receipts (paid up to date)
Approved building plan or layout plan
NOC from housing society (if applicable)
Property valuation report (bank may arrange independently)

guideContent.text: Interest Rates Overview

LAP interest rates in India currently range from 8.50% to 12.00% per annum, depending on the lender, your credit profile, and the type of property. Public sector banks like SBI and Bank of Baroda typically offer the lowest rates starting around 8.50–9.00%, while private banks and NBFCs may charge 9.50–12.00%. Most LAP loans come with floating interest rates linked to the bank's repo rate or MCLR.
LTV Ratio Explained
The Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio determines what percentage of your property's market value the bank will lend. This is a critical factor in how much you can borrow.

The actual LTV may be lower if the property is old, located in a non-preferred area, or has legal complications. Banks conduct an independent property valuation through approved assessors, and the loan amount is based on this assessed value, not the market price you expect.
LAP vs Personal Loan — Which Should You Choose?

Choose LAP when you need a large loan amount (above ₹5 Lakh) and can afford the longer processing time. The lower interest rate and extended tenure make EMIs very affordable. Choose a personal loan when you need quick disbursement for a smaller amount and do not want to put your property at risk.
Key Tips Before Applying for LAP

items: Get your property documents verified by a legal expert before approaching the bank. Title disputes can cause rejection.
Compare offers from at least 3–4 lenders. Even a 0.25% difference in interest rate saves lakhs over a long tenure.
Negotiate processing fees. Many banks are willing to reduce or waive processing fees for strong profiles.
Ensure the property has clear title and is free from any encumbrance or legal dispute.
Maintain a CIBIL score of 750+ before applying to get the best possible rate.
Calculate your FOIR before applying. If your total EMIs will exceed 50% of income, reduce existing debt first.

pages.title: How to Get Personal Loan Approved — Eligibility Tips 2026
pages.description: Expert tips to get your personal loan approved in 2026. Understand eligibility factors like income, CIBIL score, FOIR ratio, employer type, and what to do if rejected.
pages.keywords: personal loan eligibility tips, how to get personal loan approved, personal loan rejection reasons, increase personal loan eligibility, FOIR ratio personal loan 2026
pages.heroTitle: How to Get Personal Loan Approved
pages.heroSub: Understand the key eligibility factors banks check, tips to improve your chances, and what to do if your application gets rejected.

faqs.q: What is the minimum salary required for a personal loan?
faqs.a: Most banks require a minimum net monthly salary of ₹15,000–₹25,000 for personal loan eligibility. Some NBFCs accept applicants with salaries as low as ₹12,000. The actual eligible loan amount depends on your salary, existing EMIs, and the bank's FOIR limit.

faqs.q: Why was my personal loan application rejected?
faqs.a: Common rejection reasons include: CIBIL score below 700, high existing EMIs (FOIR above 50%), insufficient income, unstable employment history (less than 1 year at current job), too many recent loan inquiries, or employer not on the bank's approved list. Check your CIBIL report for errors and address the specific reason before reapplying.

faqs.q: How many times can I apply for a personal loan after rejection?
faqs.a: There is no hard limit, but each application triggers a hard inquiry that can lower your score by 5–10 points. Wait at least 3–6 months between applications. Use this time to improve your CIBIL score, reduce existing debt, and address the rejection reason.

faqs.q: Can I get a personal loan without salary slips?
faqs.a: Yes, if you are self-employed, banks accept ITR, bank statements, and business proof instead of salary slips. Some banks also offer personal loans based on strong bank account transaction history alone, even without formal income proof, though at higher interest rates.

faqs.q: Does my employer category affect personal loan eligibility?
faqs.a: Yes, significantly. Banks categorize employers into tiers — Category A (government, MNCs, Fortune 500), Category B (mid-size companies, listed firms), and Category C (small firms, startups). Category A employees get the highest loan amounts, lowest rates, and minimal documentation requirements.

guideContent.text: Key Eligibility Factors Banks Check

When you apply for a personal loan, banks run a multi-point evaluation to determine whether to approve your application and how much to offer. Understanding these factors puts you in a stronger position to secure approval at the best terms.
1. Monthly Income

Your net monthly income is the foundation of your eligibility. Banks typically require a minimum salary of ₹15,000–₹25,000 per month for salaried applicants. For self-employed individuals, annual income of ₹3–5 Lakh (as reflected in ITR) is usually the baseline. Higher income directly translates to higher eligible loan amounts because banks allow EMIs only up to a fixed percentage of your income.
2. CIBIL Score

Your CIBIL score is the most critical factor after income. Most banks set 700 as the minimum threshold for personal loans, while premium offers (lowest rates, highest amounts) are reserved for applicants with 750+. A score below 650 will result in rejection from most banks, though some NBFCs may still approve at higher interest rates (16–24% p.a.).
3. Employer Type and Category
Banks classify employers into categories that directly impact your loan terms:

rows: Highest amount, lowest rates, minimal docs
Mid-size firms, listed companies, reputed private cos
Lower amount, higher rates, more docs required

guideContent.text: If your employer is in Category A, you may receive pre-approved offers directly from your bank without even applying. Category C employees often need to provide additional income proof and may face higher interest rates.
4. Existing EMIs and Debt

Banks look at your existing monthly obligations including all running EMIs (home loan, car loan, other personal loans) and credit card minimum dues. The total of your existing EMIs plus the proposed new EMI must stay within the bank's FOIR (Fixed Obligation to Income Ratio) limit.
FOIR Ratio Explained

FOIR stands for Fixed Obligation to Income Ratio. It is the percentage of your monthly income that goes toward paying fixed obligations like EMIs. Banks typically allow a maximum FOIR of 40–55% for personal loans.

For example, if your monthly salary is ₹50,000 and the bank's FOIR limit is 50%, your maximum total EMI capacity is ₹25,000. If you already pay ₹10,000 in existing EMIs, only ₹15,000 is available for the new personal loan EMI. At 12% interest for 3 years, this translates to an eligible loan amount of approximately ₹4.5 Lakh.
Tips to Increase Your Personal Loan Eligibility
1. Improve Your CIBIL Score Before Applying

If your score is below 750, spend 3–6 months improving it before applying. Pay all bills on time, reduce credit card utilization below 30%, and avoid new credit inquiries. Even a 50-point improvement can significantly reduce your interest rate and increase the approved amount.
2. Close or Reduce Existing Loans

Paying off smaller outstanding loans (like a bike loan or an old personal loan) frees up FOIR capacity and makes you eligible for a larger amount. Even making a partial prepayment on existing loans reduces your EMI burden and improves eligibility.
3. Add a Co-Applicant or Guarantor

Some banks allow a co-applicant (usually a spouse or parent) whose income is combined with yours for eligibility. This can substantially increase the loan amount you qualify for. A guarantor with a strong credit profile can also help if your own profile is borderline.
4. Choose the Right Bank Based on Your Profile

Do not apply randomly to multiple banks. If your employer is in Category A, apply to banks where you hold a salary account for pre-approved offers. If your CIBIL score is average (650–700), consider NBFCs like Bajaj Finserv or Tata Capital that have more flexible criteria. Targeted applications reduce hard inquiries on your report.
5. Opt for a Longer Tenure

Choosing a 5-year tenure instead of 3 years reduces your monthly EMI significantly, which means the bank's FOIR calculation allows a higher loan amount. While you will pay more total interest, this strategy maximizes your approved amount if that is the priority.
6. Show Additional Income Sources

If you have rental income, freelance earnings, or investment income, provide supporting documentation. Some banks consider 50–100% of additional income when calculating eligibility. Bank statements showing regular additional credits strengthen your application.
What to Do If Your Personal Loan Is Rejected

items: Request the specific rejection reason from the bank. Under RBI guidelines, banks must provide the reason upon request.
Check your CIBIL report immediately. If the rejection was due to credit score, identify and fix the issues (errors, overdue accounts, high utilization).
Wait at least 3–6 months before reapplying. Use this time to improve your profile.
Consider alternative options: loan against FD, gold loan, or loan against mutual funds offer lower rates and easier approval.
Try a different lender. If a bank rejected you, an NBFC with more flexible criteria might approve your application.
Reduce the loan amount. Sometimes applying for a smaller amount that fits well within your FOIR can result in approval.

pages.title: Home Loan Balance Transfer — How to Save Lakhs (Step-by-Step Guide 2026)
pages.description: Complete guide to home loan balance transfer in India 2026. Learn when to transfer, how to calculate savings, documents needed, processing fees, and top banks offering lowest rates.
pages.keywords: home loan balance transfer, home loan transfer to another bank, home loan balance transfer process, home loan transfer benefits 2026, home loan switch to lower rate
pages.heroTitle: Home Loan Balance Transfer — Save Lakhs
pages.heroSub: Step-by-step guide to transferring your home loan to a lower-rate bank. Calculate your savings, understand the process, and pick the best bank.

faqs.q: What is home loan balance transfer?
faqs.a: Home loan balance transfer means moving your outstanding home loan from your current bank to another bank offering a lower interest rate. The new bank pays off your existing loan and issues a fresh loan at a reduced rate, helping you save on total interest. You continue paying EMIs to the new bank.

faqs.q: When does a home loan balance transfer make sense?
faqs.a: A balance transfer makes sense when the interest rate difference is at least 0.50% or more, you have a remaining tenure of 10 years or more, the savings after accounting for processing fees and charges exceed the transfer costs, and your current bank refuses to match the lower rate.

faqs.q: What are the charges for home loan balance transfer?
faqs.a: Charges typically include a processing fee of 0.25–1% of the outstanding loan amount, property valuation fee of Rs.2,000–Rs.5,000, legal verification charges, stamp duty on the new mortgage deed, and franking charges. Total costs usually range from Rs.10,000 to Rs.50,000 depending on the loan amount.

faqs.q: How long does a home loan balance transfer take?
faqs.a: The entire process typically takes 15–30 working days. This includes application processing (2–3 days), property valuation and legal verification (7–10 days), sanction and documentation (3–5 days), and loan disbursement and closure of old loan (5–7 days).

faqs.q: Can I get a top-up loan along with balance transfer?
faqs.a: Yes, most banks offer a top-up loan along with the balance transfer. The top-up amount depends on your property value, outstanding loan, and income. Top-up loan interest rates are usually 0.25–0.50% higher than the home loan rate, and the combined LTV must stay within 75–80%.

guideContent.text: What Is Home Loan Balance Transfer?

A home loan balance transfer is the process of switching your existing home loan from one bank or NBFC to another that offers a lower interest rate. The new lender pays off your outstanding principal to the old bank and creates a new loan account in your name. You then continue making EMI payments to the new bank at the reduced rate. This is one of the most effective ways to save lakhs of rupees over the remaining tenure of your home loan.

For example, if you have an outstanding home loan of Rs.40 Lakh at 9.50% with 15 years remaining, transferring to a bank offering 8.50% can save you approximately Rs.4.2 Lakh in total interest. The monthly EMI also drops by around Rs.2,300, improving your monthly cash flow.
When Does Balance Transfer Make Sense?
Not every borrower benefits equally from a balance transfer. The decision depends on several factors that determine whether the savings outweigh the costs involved.

items: Interest rate difference of at least 0.50% between your current rate and the new bank's offered rate.
Remaining tenure of 10 years or more. The longer the remaining tenure, the greater the savings.
Outstanding principal is substantial (Rs.20 Lakh or more). Small loans may not justify the transfer costs.
Your current bank refuses to reduce your rate when you request a rate revision.
Your credit score has improved significantly since you took the original loan, qualifying you for better rates.
You are in the early years of the loan when interest component is highest in EMIs.

guideContent.text: How to Calculate Your Savings

Before initiating a balance transfer, calculate the exact savings to ensure it is worth the effort and cost. Here is a comparison showing potential savings for different loan amounts and rate reductions.

After calculating the gross savings, subtract the transfer costs (processing fee, legal charges, stamp duty) to arrive at your net savings. If the net savings exceed Rs.50,000, the transfer is generally worthwhile.
Documents Required for Balance Transfer

items: Loan account statement from existing bank (showing outstanding principal, rate, and tenure)
List of EMIs paid (last 12 months) or bank statement showing EMI debits
Foreclosure letter or NOC request from current bank
Property documents (original title deed, sale deed, building plan)
Identity proof: PAN Card and Aadhaar Card
Income proof: Salary slips (last 3 months) or ITR (last 2 years)
Bank statements (last 6 months)
Property insurance policy (if applicable)
Passport-size photographs

guideContent.text: Processing Fees and Charges
A balance transfer involves costs from both your existing bank and the new bank. Understanding these costs is essential to calculating your true savings.

Important: Under RBI guidelines, banks cannot charge prepayment or foreclosure penalties on floating-rate home loans. This makes balance transfer cost-effective as you only pay the new bank's charges.
Top Banks for Home Loan Balance Transfer (2026)
Step-by-Step Balance Transfer Process

items: Check your current loan details: Note your outstanding principal, current interest rate, remaining tenure, and EMI amount from your loan statement.
Compare rates from other banks: Get quotes from at least 3–4 banks. Mention that you are looking for a balance transfer to negotiate better rates.
Calculate net savings: Subtract all transfer costs (processing fee, legal charges, stamp duty) from the total interest savings. Proceed only if net savings are significant.
Apply to the new bank: Submit the balance transfer application along with all required documents. The new bank will evaluate your credit profile and property.
Property valuation and legal check: The new bank will send an assessor to value your property and a legal team to verify the title documents.
Get the sanction letter: Once approved, the new bank issues a sanction letter with the new rate, tenure, and EMI details. Review all terms carefully.
Request foreclosure from old bank: Inform your current bank about the balance transfer. They must provide a foreclosure statement and release property documents.
Disbursement and closure: The new bank pays the outstanding amount directly to the old bank. Your old loan is closed and a new loan account is opened.
Mortgage registration: The property mortgage is transferred to the new bank through a fresh mortgage deed registered at the sub-registrar office.
Start EMI payments: Your new, lower EMI begins from the next month. Set up auto-debit with the new bank to ensure timely payments.

guideContent.text: Key Tips Before You Transfer

items: First ask your current bank for a rate reduction. Many banks will match competitor rates to retain you, saving you the hassle of transfer.
Transfer works best in the first half of your loan tenure when the interest component in EMIs is highest.
Maintain a CIBIL score of 750+ before applying to qualify for the lowest balance transfer rates.
Avoid taking any new loans or credit cards in the months before applying, as hard inquiries can temporarily lower your score.
Keep the tenure same or shorter after transfer. Do not extend tenure just to reduce EMI, as it increases total interest.
If you can afford higher EMIs, combine the balance transfer with a tenure reduction for maximum interest savings.

pages.title: Personal Loan vs Credit Card EMI — Which Is Cheaper? (2026 Comparison)
pages.description: Compare personal loan vs credit card EMI conversion in 2026. Interest rates, processing fees, CIBIL impact, and when to use which. Calculation examples included.
pages.keywords: personal loan vs credit card, credit card EMI vs personal loan, personal loan or credit card which is better, credit card EMI conversion charges, personal loan vs credit card interest rate 2026
pages.heroTitle: Personal Loan vs Credit Card EMI
pages.heroSub: Which is cheaper for your next big expense? A detailed comparison of interest rates, fees, and total cost with real calculation examples.

faqs.q: Is personal loan interest rate lower than credit card?
faqs.a: Yes, personal loan interest rates (10.50–24% p.a.) are significantly lower than credit card interest rates (24–42% p.a. on revolving credit). Even credit card EMI conversions charge 12–18% p.a. plus a processing fee, making personal loans the cheaper option for amounts above Rs.50,000.

faqs.q: Does converting credit card bill to EMI affect CIBIL score?
faqs.a: Converting a credit card bill to EMI does not directly hurt your CIBIL score. However, it increases your credit utilization ratio since the outstanding amount remains on your card limit. High utilization (above 30%) can negatively impact your score. A personal loan, being a separate credit line, does not affect credit card utilization.

faqs.q: When should I use credit card EMI instead of a personal loan?
faqs.a: Credit card EMI is better when the amount is small (below Rs.50,000), you need the credit immediately without a separate application process, the merchant offers 0% or low-cost EMI at checkout, or you can repay within 3–6 months. For larger amounts or longer tenures, a personal loan is almost always cheaper.

faqs.q: What are credit card EMI conversion charges?
faqs.a: Credit card EMI conversion charges typically include a processing fee of 1–2% of the converted amount plus interest of 12–18% p.a. Some banks also charge a one-time EMI conversion fee of Rs.199–Rs.499. The effective cost is often higher than a personal loan when all charges are added up.

faqs.q: Can I take a personal loan to pay off credit card debt?
faqs.a: Yes, using a personal loan to clear high-interest credit card debt is a common debt consolidation strategy. Since personal loan rates (10.50–18%) are much lower than credit card rates (24–42%), you save significantly on interest. This also simplifies repayment into a single EMI. However, ensure you do not run up credit card balances again after consolidation.

guideContent.text: Personal Loan vs Credit Card — Interest Rate Comparison

The most important difference between a personal loan and credit card financing is the interest rate. Credit cards charge some of the highest interest rates in the consumer lending market, while personal loans offer comparatively moderate rates. Understanding this difference is crucial for making the right borrowing decision.
Cost Comparison — A Real Example
Let us compare the total cost of borrowing Rs.2 Lakh through a personal loan versus credit card EMI conversion for a 12-month repayment period.
Option 1: Personal Loan at 14% p.a.

items: Loan Amount: Rs.2,00,000
Interest Rate: 14% p.a.
Tenure: 12 months
Monthly EMI: Rs.17,957
Total Interest Paid: Rs.15,484
Processing Fee (2%): Rs.4,000
Total Cost: Rs.19,484

guideContent.text: Option 2: Credit Card EMI Conversion at 15% p.a.

items: Amount Converted: Rs.2,00,000
Interest Rate: 15% p.a.
Tenure: 12 months
Monthly EMI: Rs.18,066
Total Interest Paid: Rs.16,788
Processing Fee (1.5%): Rs.3,000
EMI Conversion Fee: Rs.499
Total Cost: Rs.20,287

guideContent.text: Option 3: Credit Card Revolving Credit at 36% p.a.

items: Outstanding: Rs.2,00,000
Interest Rate: 36% p.a. (3% per month)
If paying Rs.18,000/month: Takes 13 months to clear
Total Interest Paid: Rs.38,000+
Processing Fee: Nil
Total Cost: Rs.38,000+

guideContent.text: The personal loan saves Rs.800 compared to EMI conversion and Rs.18,500 compared to revolving credit. The savings increase dramatically for larger amounts and longer tenures.
Impact on CIBIL Score
Both personal loans and credit card usage affect your CIBIL score, but in different ways. Understanding this helps you choose the option that protects your credit health.

If your credit card utilization is already above 30%, converting a large purchase to EMI will keep it elevated for the entire conversion period. A personal loan is better in this case as it does not affect your credit card utilization ratio at all.
When to Choose a Personal Loan

items: The amount needed is above Rs.50,000 and repayment will take more than 6 months.
You want the lowest possible interest rate and are willing to apply separately.
Your credit card utilization is already high and you do not want to increase it further.
You need funds in your bank account (for medical bills, education fees, etc.).
You want a fixed EMI schedule with no hidden charges.
You are consolidating multiple credit card debts into a single lower-rate loan.

guideContent.text: When to Choose Credit Card EMI

items: The amount is small (below Rs.50,000) and you can repay within 3–6 months.
The merchant is offering a no-cost EMI (0% interest) at checkout.
You need instant credit without waiting for a loan approval process.
Your credit card has a special EMI offer at a rate lower than personal loan rates.
It is a one-time purchase and you do not want the paperwork of a loan application.

guideContent.text: Key Takeaways

items: Personal loans are almost always cheaper than credit card revolving credit for any amount and tenure.
Credit card EMI conversion rates (12–18%) are closer to personal loan rates (10.50–24%), but hidden fees often make the card option slightly more expensive.
No-cost EMI offers from credit cards are the exception where card EMI beats a personal loan, but read the fine print for processing fees.
For amounts above Rs.1 Lakh, a personal loan saves thousands compared to credit card financing.
High credit card utilization hurts your CIBIL score more than a personal loan of the same amount.

pages.title: CIBIL Score for Loans — Minimum Score Required by Each Bank (2026)
pages.description: Bank-wise minimum CIBIL score required for home loan, personal loan, car loan, and more in 2026. Learn how CIBIL is calculated, factors affecting score, and how to improve it.
pages.keywords: cibil score for loan, minimum cibil score for home loan, cibil score required for personal loan, bank wise cibil score requirement, cibil score for loan approval 2026
pages.heroTitle: CIBIL Score for Loans — Bank-Wise Requirements
pages.heroSub: Minimum CIBIL score required by each bank for different loan types. Understand how your score is calculated and how to improve it quickly.

faqs.q: What is the minimum CIBIL score for a home loan?
faqs.a: Most banks require a minimum CIBIL score of 650–700 for home loan approval. SBI approves home loans with scores as low as 650, while HDFC Bank and ICICI Bank typically require 700+. A score of 750+ qualifies you for the lowest interest rates and fastest approval across all banks.

faqs.q: Can I get a loan with a CIBIL score of 600?
faqs.a: Getting a loan with a 600 CIBIL score is difficult with mainstream banks but possible through NBFCs like Bajaj Finserv, Tata Capital, and L&T Finance. The interest rate will be 3–6% higher than what a 750+ applicant would get. Secured loans like gold loans or loans against FD are easier to get with lower scores.

faqs.q: How is CIBIL score calculated?
faqs.a: CIBIL score is calculated based on five factors: payment history (35% weightage), credit utilization (30%), credit age or length of credit history (15%), credit mix of secured and unsecured loans (10%), and number of recent credit inquiries (10%). Payment history and utilization together account for 65% of your score.

faqs.q: How long does it take to improve CIBIL score from 650 to 750?
faqs.a: Improving your CIBIL score from 650 to 750 typically takes 4–8 months of disciplined credit behaviour. Pay all EMIs and credit card bills on time, reduce credit utilization below 30%, clear any overdue accounts, and avoid new credit applications. The exact timeline depends on what is pulling your score down.

faqs.q: Does checking CIBIL score reduce it?
faqs.a: No, checking your own CIBIL score is a soft inquiry and has zero impact on your score. You can check it as many times as you want. Only hard inquiries made by banks when you apply for a loan or credit card can reduce your score by 5–10 points per inquiry.

guideContent.text: Bank-Wise Minimum CIBIL Score for Loans (2026)

Different banks have different minimum CIBIL score requirements depending on the loan type, amount, and your overall profile. Here is a comprehensive bank-wise breakdown for the most common loan types.
Home Loan — Minimum CIBIL Score
Personal Loan — Minimum CIBIL Score
Car Loan — Minimum CIBIL Score
How Is CIBIL Score Calculated?

Your CIBIL score is a three-digit number between 300 and 900, calculated by TransUnion CIBIL based on your credit behaviour reported by banks and NBFCs. The score is determined by five key factors, each carrying a different weightage.

rows: On-time payments of EMIs and credit card bills
Percentage of available credit limit being used
Average age of all your credit accounts
Diversity of credit types (secured vs unsecured)
Number of hard inquiries in recent months

guideContent.text: Payment history is the single most important factor. Even one missed EMI or credit card payment can drop your score by 50–100 points. Credit utilization (keeping balances below 30% of your credit card limit) is the second most impactful factor and is something you can improve quickly.
Factors That Can Damage Your CIBIL Score

items: Late or missed EMI payments: A single 30-day late payment can drop your score by 50–100 points.
High credit card utilization: Using more than 30% of your credit limit signals credit hunger.
Multiple loan applications: Each application triggers a hard inquiry, reducing your score by 5–10 points.
Loan settlement or write-off: Settling a loan for less than the full amount is treated as a negative mark for 7 years.
Becoming a guarantor for a defaulter: If the primary borrower defaults, it damages your score too.
Closing old credit accounts: Reduces your average credit age and total available credit.
Having only unsecured credit: A mix of secured (home/car loan) and unsecured (personal loan/credit card) is preferred.

guideContent.text: How to Check Your CIBIL Score for Free

items: CIBIL Official Website: Visit myscore.cibil.com for 1 free credit report per year as mandated by RBI.
Bank Mobile Apps: SBI YONO, HDFC Bank, ICICI iMobile, and Axis Bank apps provide free CIBIL score checks for account holders.
Third-Party Platforms: Paytm, PhonePe, BankBazaar, and Paisabazaar offer free credit score monitoring with monthly updates.
RBI Mandate: Under RBI guidelines, all four credit bureaus (CIBIL, Experian, Equifax, CRIF High Mark) must provide one free report per year.

guideContent.text: How to Improve Your CIBIL Score Quickly
If your CIBIL score is below the required minimum for the loan you want, here are the fastest and most effective steps to improve it.

rows: Pay all overdue EMIs and bills
Reduce credit card utilization to below 30%
Dispute and fix errors in CIBIL report
Build history with secured credit card

guideContent.text: The fastest improvement comes from clearing overdue payments and reducing credit card balances. These two actions alone can boost your score by 70–140 points within 1–2 months. For the best results, combine all the actions above consistently over 3–6 months.
CIBIL Score Ranges and Loan Impact

A score of 750 and above is considered the gold standard for loan approval. At this level, you get the lowest interest rates, highest loan amounts, fastest processing, and minimal documentation requirements. Scores between 700–749 still get approved easily but at slightly higher rates. Below 650, most mainstream banks will decline your application, and you will need to approach NBFCs or opt for secured loans.

pages.title: Education Loan in India 2026 — Complete Guide (Eligibility, Banks, Interest Rates)
pages.description: Complete guide to education loans in India 2026. Compare interest rates by bank, learn about collateral requirements, moratorium period, tax benefits under Section 80E, and top banks.
pages.keywords: education loan india 2026, education loan interest rate, education loan eligibility, education loan for abroad, education loan tax benefit section 80E, best bank for education loan
pages.heroTitle: Education Loan in India 2026 — Complete Guide
pages.heroSub: Everything you need to know about education loans: types, interest rates, collateral rules, moratorium period, tax benefits, and the best banks to apply.

faqs.q: What is the interest rate for education loans in 2026?
faqs.a: Education loan interest rates in India range from 8.15% to 13.50% p.a. in 2026. Public sector banks like SBI (8.15–10.65%), Bank of Baroda (8.30–10.50%), and PNB (8.45–10.80%) offer the lowest rates. Private banks and NBFCs charge 9.50–13.50%. Rates depend on loan amount, collateral, and whether the course is domestic or abroad.

faqs.q: Do I need collateral for an education loan?
faqs.a: Education loans up to Rs.7.5 Lakh from most banks do not require collateral. Loans between Rs.7.5 Lakh and Rs.20 Lakh may require a co-obligant but no collateral. Loans above Rs.20 Lakh typically require collateral such as property, fixed deposit, or other approved securities. Government banks are generally more lenient on collateral requirements.

faqs.q: What is the moratorium period for education loans?
faqs.a: The moratorium period is the grace period during which you do not need to repay the loan. It typically covers the course duration plus 6–12 months after completing the course (or 6 months after getting a job, whichever is earlier). During moratorium, interest continues to accrue and gets added to the principal (capitalized) unless you choose to pay simple interest during this period.

faqs.q: Can I claim tax benefit on education loan?
faqs.a: Yes, under Section 80E of the Income Tax Act, the interest paid on an education loan is fully deductible from your taxable income. There is no upper limit on the deduction amount. The benefit is available for a maximum of 8 years from the year you start repaying the loan, or until the interest is fully repaid, whichever is earlier. Only the student or parent who repays the loan can claim this deduction.

faqs.q: Which is the best bank for education loan in India?
faqs.a: SBI offers the lowest interest rates (8.15% onwards) and has the widest reach for education loans in India. Bank of Baroda and Canara Bank are also excellent choices for domestic courses. For studying abroad, SBI Global Ed-Vantage and Credila (HDFC subsidiary) are popular. Choose based on your loan amount, course, and whether you have collateral.

guideContent.text: Types of Education Loans in India

Education loans in India can be broadly classified based on the course location, loan amount, and collateral requirement. Understanding these categories helps you pick the right loan product and bank.

rows: No collateral, co-obligant needed, lower processing
Property or FD as collateral, lower rates

guideContent.text: Education Loan Interest Rates by Bank (2026)
Interest rates vary significantly across banks and depend on the loan amount, collateral availability, and course type. Here is a comparison of the top banks offering education loans in India.

Public sector banks (SBI, Bank of Baroda, PNB, Canara Bank) consistently offer the lowest rates but may have longer processing times. Private banks and NBFCs like HDFC Credila offer faster processing and higher loan amounts for premier institutions.
Collateral Requirements

Collateral is a security pledge you provide to the bank to reduce their lending risk. For education loans, collateral rules are standardized by the Indian Banks Association (IBA) guidelines, though individual banks may vary.

items: Loans up to Rs.4 Lakh: No security, no co-obligant required.
Loans Rs.4 Lakh to Rs.7.5 Lakh: Co-obligant (parent or guardian) required, no collateral.
Loans Rs.7.5 Lakh to Rs.20 Lakh: Co-obligant required. Some banks may ask for third-party guarantee.
Loans above Rs.20 Lakh: Tangible collateral required — residential or commercial property, fixed deposits, government securities, or LIC policies.
Accepted collateral types: Residential property (most preferred), commercial property, agricultural land (some banks), fixed deposits, NSC/KVP certificates, and LIC policies.

guideContent.text: Moratorium Period Explained

The moratorium period is one of the most borrower-friendly features of education loans. During this period, you are not required to pay EMIs, giving you time to complete your education and find employment before repayment begins.

items: Duration: Covers the entire course period plus 6–12 months after course completion (or 6 months after getting a job, whichever is earlier).
Interest during moratorium: Interest continues to accrue on the outstanding loan amount. This interest gets capitalized (added to the principal) at the end of the moratorium period.
Simple interest option: Many banks allow you to pay only the interest during the moratorium period. This prevents interest capitalization and reduces your total repayment burden significantly.

Example: On a Rs.10 Lakh loan at 9% for a 4-year course, the interest accumulated during the 5-year moratorium period would be approximately Rs.4.5 Lakh. If capitalized, your total repayment principal becomes Rs.14.5 Lakh.

guideContent.text: Pro tip: If you can afford it, pay at least the simple interest during the moratorium period. This can save you Rs.1–3 Lakh in total interest over the loan tenure.
Tax Benefits Under Section 80E

Education loans come with significant tax benefits that can reduce your effective cost of borrowing. Section 80E of the Income Tax Act allows you to deduct the entire interest paid on an education loan from your taxable income.

items: Deduction available: The entire interest portion of your education loan EMI is deductible. There is no upper limit on the amount.
Duration: The deduction is available for a maximum of 8 consecutive years, starting from the year you begin repaying the loan.
Who can claim: The person who repays the loan can claim the deduction — this can be the student or the parent.
Eligible courses: Full-time courses in India or abroad at recognized institutions, including graduation, post-graduation, professional courses, and vocational programs.

Principal repayment: The principal portion is not deductible under Section 80E. However, the principal repaid on a loan taken for self-education may be claimed under Section 80C (up to Rs.1.5 Lakh combined limit).

guideContent.text: Tax Savings Example
Eligibility Criteria for Education Loans
Education loan eligibility depends on the student's academic profile, the institution, and the co-applicant's financial standing.

items: Student must be an Indian citizen.
Must have secured admission to a recognized institution in India or abroad through merit-based selection (entrance exam, merit list, etc.).
Age: Typically 18–35 years at the time of loan application.
Academic record: Most banks require 50%+ marks in the qualifying examination. Premier institutions may have relaxed academic criteria.
Co-applicant: Parent, guardian, or spouse is mandatory as co-obligant for most education loans.
Co-applicant income: No fixed minimum, but the co-applicant should demonstrate the ability to repay if the student cannot.
Eligible courses: Graduation, post-graduation, professional courses (engineering, medicine, MBA, law, etc.), and approved vocational courses.

guideContent.text: Step-by-Step Application Process

items: Secure admission: Get your admission confirmation letter and fee structure from the institution. Banks will not process loans without a confirmed admission.
Research and compare banks: Compare interest rates, processing fees, moratorium periods, and collateral requirements across 3–4 banks. Prioritize government banks for lower rates.
Gather documents: Prepare admission letter, fee structure, mark sheets, identity and address proof (student and co-applicant), income proof of co-applicant, and collateral documents (if applicable).
Apply online or visit branch: Most banks offer online education loan applications. You can also visit the nearest branch with all documents for in-person assistance.
Bank evaluation: The bank evaluates the institution's reputation, course value, co-applicant's repayment capacity, and collateral (if applicable). This takes 5–15 working days.
Sanction and acceptance: Once approved, the bank issues a sanction letter detailing the loan amount, interest rate, moratorium period, and repayment terms. Review all conditions before accepting.
Disbursement: The loan is disbursed directly to the educational institution in stages — typically one disbursement per semester or year, as per the fee schedule.
Repayment begins: After the moratorium period ends, EMI payments start. Set up auto-debit to ensure you never miss a payment.

guideContent.text: Key Tips for Education Loan Applicants

items: Apply early: Start the loan application process 2–3 months before the fee payment deadline to avoid last-minute stress.
Pay simple interest during moratorium if possible. It can save Rs.1–5 Lakh over the loan tenure.

Government schemes: Check eligibility for Central Sector Interest Subsidy Scheme (CSIS) which provides full interest subsidy during the moratorium period for students from families with annual income below Rs.4.5 Lakh.
Vidya Lakshmi Portal: Use the Government of India's Vidya Lakshmi portal (vidyalakshmi.co.in) to apply to multiple banks with a single application.
Compare total cost, not just interest rate. Factor in processing fees, insurance requirements, and prepayment charges.
Start repaying early if you get a job or scholarship. Partial payments during the moratorium reduce the capitalized interest significantly.
